title: Travel Survival: Commands, Directions, and Negotiation
learningGoal: Learn how to give direct commands (imperatives) using all three formality levels, negotiate prices with shopkeepers, and navigate directions effectively in India.
instructions: This block is your ultimate survival guide for traveling in India. You will learn the 'Imperative Mood'—how to tell people what to do. You will also learn the core vocabulary needed to survive an auto-rickshaw ride and a bargaining session at a market.

lessonContent.bodyText:
When you want to tell someone to do something (e.g., 'Stop here!', 'Come inside!', 'Look!'), you use an imperative verb. 

Because Hindi has three levels of 'You' (Tu, Tum, Aap), there are THREE ways to give every command. Let's use the verb 'Dekhna' (To look/see):

1. Extremely Informal (Tu): Drop the 'na'. 
- Example: Dekh! (Look! - to a close friend or a dog).

2. Casual (Tum): Replace 'na' with 'o'. 
- Example: Dekho! (Look! - to a friend or younger person).

3. Formal (Aap): Replace 'na' with 'iye'.
- Example: Dekhiye! (Look! / Please look! - to an elder, shopkeeper, or stranger).

targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: Stop here. (Casual - speaking to an auto driver)
Please come inside. (Formal - welcoming a guest)

lessonContent.bodyText:
When traveling, you will constantly use the 'Aap' (formal) or 'Tum' (casual) commands. It is highly recommended that foreigners stick to 'Aap' commands (ending in -iye) to show respect and avoid conflict.

Key Verbs for Travel:
- Rokhna (To stop) -> Rokiye! (Please stop!)
- Jaana (To go) -> Jaiye! (Please go!)
- Aana (To come) -> Aaiye! (Please come!)
- Dena (To give) -> Deejiye! (Please give! *Irregular*)
- Lena (To take) -> Leejiye! (Please take! *Irregular*)
- Karna (To do) -> Keejiye! (Please do! *Irregular*)

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: मुझे एक पानी की बोतल दीजिए।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Mujhe ek paani ki botal deejiye.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: Please give me a water bottle.

lessonContent.bodyText:
If you are in a taxi or auto-rickshaw, you need to guide the driver. The Hindi words for directions are essential:

- Seedha (सीधा): Straight
- Daayein (दाएँ): Right
- Baayein (बाएँ): Left
- Aage (आगे): Ahead / Forward
- Peeche (पीछे): Behind / Back
- Yahaan (यहाँ): Here
- Wahaan (वहाँ): There

To tell someone to turn, you say '[Direction] lijiye' (Take [Direction]).

Shopping in local Indian markets requires negotiation. If you speak in Hindi, you will get a much better price than if you speak in English.

Phrases for Bargaining:
- Kitne ka hai? (कितने का है?): How much is this for?
- Bahut mehenga hai! (बहुत महँगा है!): It is very expensive!
- Thoda kam keejiye. (थोड़ा कम कीजिए।): Please reduce it a bit. (Literally: Do a little less).
- Sahi daam lagaiye. (सही दाम लगाइए।): Apply the right price.
- Kitna hua? (कितना हुआ?): How much did it become? (Used to ask the total bill).

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: भैया, यह बहुत महँगा है। थोड़ा कम कीजिए।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Bhaiya, yeh bahut mehenga hai. Thoda kam keejiye.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: Brother, this is very expensive. Please reduce it a little.
How much for both of them?

lessonContent.bodyText:
To tell someone NOT to do something, we use 'Mat' (मत) instead of 'Nahi'.

'Nahi' is for stating facts (I am not going). 'Mat' is for giving orders (Do not go!).

Place 'Mat' directly before the verb.

Examples:
- Casual: Wahaan mat jao! (Don't go there!)
- Formal: Aisa mat keejiye. (Please don't do like this.)
- Intimate: Mat bol! (Don't speak!)

In India, you almost never use 'Excuse me' or 'Sir'. Instead, you create a makeshift familial relationship with the service worker.

- Bhaiya (भैया - Brother): The universal term for shopkeepers, auto drivers, waiters, and any male roughly your age or older. 
- Didi (दीदी - Older Sister): The universal term for female workers or helpers.
- Uncle / Aunty: Used for people significantly older than you.
- Suniye! (सुनिए - Please listen!): The direct translation of 'Excuse me' to grab a waiter's or stranger's attention.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: सुनिए भैया, लाल किला कहाँ है?
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Suniye bhaiya, Lal Qila kahan hai?
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: Excuse me brother, where is the Red Fort?

learningObjective: The learner will confidently navigate Indian streets by utilizing imperative verbs to give directions, employing culturally appropriate honorifics (Bhaiya/Didi), and negotiating prices using standard market phrases.

examples.targetText: आगे से दाएँ लीजिए, फिर सीधा चलिए।
examples.transliteration: Aage se daayein leejiye, phir seedha chaliye.
examples.baseTranslation: Take a right from ahead, then keep going straight.
examples.explanation: Uses formal imperatives 'leejiye' (take) and 'chaliye' (go/walk) alongside direction words.

examples.targetText: पाँच सौ रुपये बहुत ज़्यादा हैं। सही दाम लगाइए।
examples.transliteration: Paanch sau rupaye bahut zyada hain. Sahi daam lagaiye.
examples.baseTranslation: Five hundred rupees is too much. Apply the right price.
examples.explanation: A classic negotiation phrase. 'Zyada' means much/excess. 'Lagaiye' is the formal command for 'apply'.

importantRules.ruleText: Always use 'Mat' instead of 'Nahi' for negative commands.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Saying 'Nahi jao' sounds like broken Hindi. It must be 'Mat jao'.

importantRules.ruleText: The verbs Dena (give), Lena (take), Karna (do), and Peena (drink) have irregular formal imperatives: Deejiye, Leejiye, Keejiye, Peejiye.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Do not say 'Deniye' or 'Kariye'. Memorize these four exceptions as they are extremely common.

cultureContext.contextText: Calling a shopkeeper 'Bhaiya' instantly humanizes the interaction. It is not overly familiar; it is the cultural standard. If you combine 'Bhaiya' with formal 'Aap' commands (like Deejiye), you strike the perfect balance of respect and approachability.
dosList: Use 'Bhaiya' for waiters, drivers, and vendors.
Use 'Suniye' to get someone's attention respectfully.
dontsList: Avoid using 'Tu' commands (e.g., Ruk! Chal!) with auto drivers unless they are being actively aggressive. It can easily escalate a conflict.

summary: You are now equipped for the streets of India. You know how to command a taxi driver to go straight (Seedha chaliye) or stop (Rokiye). You can haggle at the market (Thoda kam keejiye). And most importantly, you know to use 'Mat' for negative orders (Aisa mat keejiye).

checkpointQuestions.questionText: What is the formal 'Aap' command for 'To go' (Jaana)?
If you want to tell someone NOT to look, which negative word do you use before the verb?
What does 'Aage se baayein leejiye' mean?
What is the irregular formal command for 'To Give' (Dena)?

keyTakeaways: Commands: Drop 'na'. Tu = Root. Tum = add 'o'. Aap = add 'iye'.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: Mastery of the imperative mood, directions, and market negotiation vocabulary.
importantFacts: Bhaiya is the universal respectful term for service workers.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: Learners can navigate transport and markets independently using culturally appropriate commands.

feedbackRules.onPass: Fantastic! You are fully prepared to survive an Indian marketplace or auto ride.
feedbackRules.onFail: Review Section 1 and 2. Remember the endings: Tu = Root, Tum = O, Aap = Iye.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good progress. Don't forget the 4 irregular verbs: Deejiye, Leejiye, Keejiye, Peejiye. They don't follow the normal 'iye' rule!

audioAssets.audioTranscript: यहाँ रुको। अंदर आइये। भैया, गाड़ी रोकिए। मुझे एक पानी की बोतल दीजिए। आगे से बाएँ लीजिए। बस, यहीं रोकिए। भैया, यह बहुत महँगा है। थोड़ा कम कीजिए। दोनों का कितना हुआ? मुझे मत छुओ! चिंता मत कीजिए। सुनिए भैया, लाल किला कहाँ है?

hintText: Whenever you are in doubt, add 'iye' to the verb root. It is the safest and most polite way to speak.
commonMistakes: Using 'Nahi' instead of 'Mat' for commands (e.g., 'Nahi jao').
Saying 'Kariye' instead of the correct irregular 'Keejiye'.
